messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 Measures to maximise impact - Dissemination, exploitation and communication Our RECREATE strategy for Communication, Dissemination & Exploitation (CDE) aims at supporting the achievement of the Expected Outcomes, within our project lifetime, and facilitating the achievement of the medium-long term Wider Impacts, beyond our project. Our communication activities will generate awareness of our project among different stakeholders and interest in obtaining the disseminated results. The management of research data and IPR will guide our dissemination activities to serve the academic community while protecting our consortium’s interest in commercial exploitation. Dissemination and exploitation of our project results will be based on large-scale demonstration action of cross-sectorial circular value-chains for the recovery and re-use of FRC into high added value products. Among the main existing barriers, our D&E actions will address (i) the lack of circular business models for boosting profitability; (ii) the poor customer acceptance for remanufactured products. Main goals of our CDE workpackages (WP7 -8) will be (i) facilitating the industrial uptake within local eco-systems; (ii) de-risking future industrial uptake through private investments; (iii) increasing awareness for a more conscious consumer behaviour. Large-scale replication and multiplication will take advantage of the Vanguard Initiative “De-and Remanufacturing for Circular Economy” pilot network. 

2.2.1 Exploitation of our project results Our CDE plan and the related process will be led by G_MEDIA, our RECREATE CDE Manager (Section 3). The company has consolidated expertise in engaging, training and supporting capacity building with relevant stakeholders brought to the dedicated unit Circular Business Academy (www.circularbusiness.academy) and to the development of the Roadmap Towards the Circular Economy in Slovenia. In the implementation of the exploitation part of our plan (WP8), our RECREATE exploitation team will coordinate us and define the organisational aspects needed to address the market. The process will be based on 4 workshops, namely “WS1: Industrial thematic workshops with potential stakeholders”, “WS2: KER exploitation workshop and dedicated exploitation session”, “WS3: Exploitation implementation workshops”, “WS4: How to pitch” workshop. These workshops will provide each demo-case with the opportunity to work on (i) the identification/grouping of key exploitable results; (ii) the first definition of the related use mode; (iii) the identification and mapping of risks related to the exploitation; (iv) follow-up actions. These WS will follow the path tracked by the Lean Canvas approach that will be used for the Business and Use Model Development of the KERs. A crucial step for exploitation is the identification of the risk factors hampering future use, the evaluation and prioritization of the risk factors and the identification of actions to minimize their impact. Risk factors will be screened following these categories: Partnership, Technological, Market, IPR, Environmental and Financial risks. We aim at fostering competitiveness and growth and increasing benefits to us and to the EU economy and citizens. Depending on the project result type, we have developed an innovative method for identifying KERs in a comprehensive and systemic way, targeting different exploitation routes customized to the specific demonstration strategies. Moreover, we have analyzed all the exploitable solutions generated at value-chain level and identified four stakeholder groups for exploitation: 

• Technology and B2B-service providers – TP - and Design & Engineering groups – DE -, which are interested in selling the solutions (or the use of the solutions) and new designs developed in RECREATE to the largest possible target end-user groups, contributing to a sustainable transition to Circular Economy. 
• Recyclers and Remanufacturers – RR - (Lead-users), recycling companies and remanufacturing/repair companies interested in using the new demand-driven recovery and remanufacturing solutions developed by RECREATE for (i) selling recovered components/materials to end-users (ii) selling services to end-users. 
• Product Manufacturers and B2C -service providers – MFG - (End-users): The manufacturers, which are interested in making a concrete transition towards circular economy business models which can enhance the ability to decrease the costs of composite materials, to propose new customized “green” products to the market, and to contribute to the environmental sustainability of their production processes. 

According to our project goals, the main exploitable solutions, and the respective exploitation plan will be as reported in Table 8. 

Table 8 Preliminary identification of KERs and exploitation routes. 

Exploitable products Main Exploiter(s) Route to exploitation Time to market Target Industry 
During RECREATE After RECREATE 
New products offered at value-chain level Added-value, customized products re-using fibers and resins. TP: POLIMI, GAIKER, TAU, IRIS. RR: Carbon Cleanup, COBAT COMPOSITI. MFG: GEVEN SPA, HEAD Sport GmbH, BENASEDO. We will test the effectiveness of the developed solutions and will demonstrate the benefits at pilot scale in 5 demo-cases. Our RECREATE demo-cases will be translated into new products. Extensions to other markets will be considered according to the output of our exploitation strategy. 4 years after our project ends. Form: internal product development, RTOs spin-offs. 
• Furniture 
• Automotive 
• Sport equipment 
• Design 
• Appliances. 

• Consumer goods FRP components modular design, reman., repair, reforming, and reuse TP: Fraunhofer, ICAM, CNRS, IRIS, INV, POLIMI DE: REST UG, EDAG. RR: COBAT COMPOSITI MFG: EDAG, REST UG, GEVEN SPA We will bring the modular design, disassembly, remanufacturing and reforming processes at TRL 4-6 and will test the properties of the remanufactured components. MFGs have strategic interest in opening new markets for this attractive circular economy solution, after validation by customers and business model refining. 4 years after our project ends. Form: patenting. 
• Wind energy 
• Marine 
• Aeronautics 
• Automotive 
• Furniture 

New sustainable-by-design composite materials with enhanced functionalities TP: POLIMI, INVENT, EDAG. DE: EDAG. RR: COBAT COMPOSITI. MFG: EDAG, REST UG We will develop and demonstrate solutions for sustainable-by-design materials including by (i) new green reversible resins, (ii) reversible joining, and (iii) modular structures. These solutions will be transferred to the industry at large scale through dedicated dissemination workshops, in order to investigate potential market uptakes in different sectors. 4 years after our project ends. Form: patenting. 
• Wind energy 
• Marine 
• Aeronautics 
• Automotive 
• Furniture 

New software technical enablers. MULO (Make, Use, Licensing, Outsourcing) Circular Economy digital platform for supporting the adoption of products re-using materials and components M, L: G_MEDIA, UPAT, GRIFO U: All project partners. We will design and develop core data structure and services to support value chain integration and collaborative development of new products using composite. Our partners will have strategic interest in promoting the platform and push for the replication, by large system integrator and services providers, in different markets. 2 years after our project ends. Form: packaging of services of the platform. 
• All sectors of RECREATE 
• Customers, 
• Investors, 
• Waste mgmt. consortia. 
• Municipalities. 
• Regions 

Digitalisation of knowledge about new materials, products and innovative processes for composite re-use for training tools and gamification. M, L: POLIMI, GRIFO U: all stakeholders, including end-customers, service providers, policy makers, regulators, standardization and certification bodies. We will develop a large pool of digitalized knowledge, including data, pictures, KPI figures, validation data, description of materials, designs, testing, certifications etc. for all the demonstrated products and best practices on how to conduct a successful replication. POLIMI and other scientific partners will continuously update the digital platform database and the set of collected examples and will provide access to the library to the EU stakeholders. GRIFO will design services to make the digital learning tools available to external users. 1 year after our project ends. Form: Licensing. 
• Composite production. 
• Composite reuse industry. 
• Public authorities, certification and standardisation bodies 

2.2.1.1 Preliminary Business Plans for our RECREATE Exploitable Products and Enabling Technologies Individual Partner Business Plan – HEAD Sport GmbH: Our main industrial interest is to validate the re-use of CFs in padel and pickle-ball rackets (Table 9). The flat surfaces require isotropic properties and the use of recycled material from the early beginning of these growing markets will set the standards. Yet the technical requirements of these impact sports remain high and the material needs to comply with them. The result must be highly competitive against current solutions under development. It is assumed the lower costs of the material allow additional production steps necessary for working with recycled fibers. For recycled GFs, our interest is to replace a fleece with rGFs, which is in nearly every ski produced. The fleece must provide interlaminar strength. To achieve this, there is a potential rGF use of 20-25% of total GF volume used. The introduction of recycled materials should not exceed current target cost. 

Table 9 Potential economic benefits for our HEAD Sport GmbH demo-cases. 

Benefits RECREATE +1 years +2 years +3 years +4 years +5 years 
Sales skis [n° units] 50.000 100.000 100.000

You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Detail the dissemination plan, exploitation roadmap, IP strategy, and open access commitments.
Write the Dissemination, exploitation and communication section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

2.2 Measures to Maximise Impact – Dissemination, Exploitation, Communication    
2.2.1 Impact Strategy  
We will develop an integrated dissemination, exploitation, and communication (DEC) strategy (Figure 2.2a) designed to maximise impact and to be sufficiently flexible so as to effectively address the different target groups for the solutions we propose. Our impact strategy aims to maximise the legacy of our project and the concrete actions will be outlined in the Dissemination, Exploitation and Communication Master Plan DECMP (D 7.1). This strategy paper will target stakeholders of the proposed value chain, such as AD operators, microalgae producers, biorefinery technology manufacturers, sensor system developers, aviation fuel/SAF producers, aviation industry stakeholders, potential by-product users (e.g. farmers, fertiliser manufacturers), academic community, fuel certification authorities, investors, policy makers, journalists and media companies, and consumers (i.e. people who fly). We will elaborate the following key DEC goals:  
Goal 1: raising awareness and informing target groups of importance and potential of microalgae alternative fuels  
Goal 2: engaging in a dialogue with stakeholders along the algae value chain  
Goal 3: enabling knowledge transfer amongst us on specific designs, data sets, and methodologies;  
Goal 4: fostering exploitation opportunities for further research, policy and commercial adaptation.  
[HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels]  
Dissemination and training strategy (Figure 2.2a): We will select (1) the most suited journals, topics and events to publish the scientific results and reach relevant audience. They will be presented in (2) selected Open-Access outlets and conferences, while fostering collaboration and engagement with relevant target groups. A mapping exercise will be carried out to identify key EU projects relevant to the area (Task 7.3), such as those funded by HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08, HORIZON-CL5-2022-D3-03-07, HORIZON-CL4-2021-RESILIENCE-01-16, and REFOLUTION, FLITE, and ALIGHT. We will make a proactive approach to connect with these projects to promote mutual project workshops and seminars, with the view of organising joint seminars/workshops to expand the network and reach of dissemination activities. In addition, students and young researchers will benefit from a Research Summer School in microalgae fuel dedicated lecture series at UCD, UCAM and KIT. Different stakeholders will also get the opportunity to engage with us through regular demonstration workshops at the pilot-scale algae fuel production facility that will be built at BIAE during our project lifetime. The evaluation of these actions (3) will be done through the review of citations, impact factor of journals, altimetric and feedback forms.  
Exploitation strategy (Figure 2.2a): We will (4) identify Key Exploitable Results (KERs), target markets, potential users or clients and define the IP Management Plan in our Consortium Agreement. This forms the basis for developing potential (5) exploitation pathways and associated IPR strategy. By (6) monitoring the market, we will review the exploitation strategy and refine it throughout our project. We will also deliver a commercialisation model (D7.4) and policy white paper (D7.5) in order to harness our project results, facilitate commercialisation and legislative adoption by relevant stakeholders beyond our project lifetime.  
Communication strategy (Figure 2.2a): A multi-layer communication strategy will support and complement the dissemination and exploitation activities, and will include (7) identification of appropriate communication channels and formulation of key messages for the various target groups. This plan will then be implemented to produce (8) original content including journalistic articles, press releases, videos, infographics and social media posts. Monitoring tools (9) will measure the outreach of these actions and, while some actions may achieve a better outreach than expected (and can be repeated), other actions that are less successful can be adjusted.  
2.2.2 Dissemination + Training Actions  
Table 2.2a – Planned dissemination actions for our project  
Industry workshops and networking events, webinars, and seminars  
(Some will be organised as joint events with other EU projects in the area)  
Goals 1-4 M13 – M48  
Key motivation: run industry/end-user workshops, webinars, seminars to engage stakeholders. Some of these events will be jointly organised with EU projects in similar microalgae, fuel, aviation areas. Target audience: AD operators, algae producers, fuel/SAF producers, aviation sector, by-product users, sensor manufacturers, industry associations (e.g. EABA), investors, university students, air passengers. KPIs: 20+ events, reaching 800+ stakeholders  
Workshops at pilot-scale algae fuel facility on AD site (Kilshane, Ireland) Goals 1-4 M37 – M48  
Key motivation: run demonstration workshops to engage stakeholders. Target audience: AD operators, algae producers, fuel/SAF producers, aviation sector, by-product users, sensor manufacturers, fuel certification authorities, industry associations (e.g. EABA), policymakers, investors, university students. KPIs: 4 workshops, reaching 800+ stakeholders  
Scientific publications (open access) Goals 1,3,4 M13 – M48  
Key motivation: exchange scientific knowledge, provide technical details and open data  
Target audience: Academic community; Potential Journals (Impact Factor): Algal Research (IF = 5.276), Bioresource Technology (IF = 11.889), Chemical Engineering Journal (IF = 16.744), Frontiers in Energy Research (IF = 3.858). KPIs: >10+ peer-reviewed journal publications, reaching > 500 academics & leading to > 150 citations  
Conference presentations and fairs Goals 1-4 M13 – M48  
Key motivation: present scientific methodologies, results, and their impact; Target audience: AD operators, algae producers, fuel/SAF producers, aviation sector, academic community; Potential Conferences: AlgaEurope, European Algae Industry Summit (ACI); KPIs: >12 presentations and >2 conference workshops reaching > 500 experts  
Microalgae Fuel Summer Research School Goals 1-4 M25 – M48  
Key motivation: provide summer research internships in microalgae fuel production with dedicated lectures on the technologies and methodologies developed in our project; Target audience: University students; KPIs: 2-8 weeks combined research internship and lecture sessions for 5-15 students, to be jointly coordinated/implemented by UCD, UCAM and KIT.  
Technical Brochure Goals 1-4 M45– M48  
Figure 2.2a - Impact strategy of our project  
[HORIZON-CL5-2023-D3-02-08] [Development of microalgae fuel production and purification technologies for advanced aviation fuels]  
2.2.3 Exploitation Action and IP Management  
We will implement exploitation plans (WP7) to optimise the impact of our project’s results after project completion. Exploitation will focus on creating new academic and commercial opportunities for algae fuel technologies and delivering fact-based information on renewable fuel technologies for policymakers. Our project is set up in such a way that the future opportunities for the exploitation of our project’s results can be maximised, as the technology feasibility (demonstrated at TRL4/5) will be complemented with techno-economic and life-cycle analysis to assess scalability. The 11 Key Exploitable Results (KERs), comprised of novel methodologies and technologies, are listed in Table 2.2b. We will identify exploitation means for each KER (e.g. in terms of licensing, spin-outs, or direct use) with the long-term ambition to contribute to the route to TRL9.  
Table 2.2b – Our KERs and summary of IP strategies by each partner  
KER Owner Means of exploitation and/or protection  
KER1: Autotrophic growth strategy on digestate A4F  
Utilisation of KER1 for the development of a new service for A4F’s clients based on the design, build operation and transfer (DBOT) of microalgae cultivation units using digestate.  
KER2: Membrane system for CO2 delivery to microalgae culture UCD  
Secure IP protection & pursue commercialisation activities via Technology Transfer Office & NovaUCD (innovation hub with experience in supporting 360 start-ups-€760M turnover- from 2004-2019). KER2 will consolidate UCD’s growing knowledge basis in microalgae biotechnology to support formation of Irish microalgal spin-out specialising in wastewater treatment.  
KER3: In-line sensor for bacterial detection/enumeration IRIS  
Further develop, secure IP and commercialise the pioneering Multirange Spectroscopic Platform (‘Fusion’) for in-line monitoring of complex samples in Industry 4.0. KER3 will demonstrate the platform’s capacity for microalgae and open new market avenues.  
KER4: UV irradiation system for bacterial control UCD  
KER4 and KER5 will consolidate UCD’s knowledge basis in microalgae for wastewater treatment and biorefinery production of valuable products. Same exploitation pathways as KER2.  
KER5: Low-energy cell disruption based on autolysis  
KER6: Lipid extraction strategy to minimise solvent KIT  
Secure IP protection through technology transfer division responsible for commercialisation. Gained knowledge on solvent system and overall process design (KER6) will be made available to equipment manufacturers.  
KER
